impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that the tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its '844 patent (covering fuel vapor canister systems) on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's patent-law defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors of dominant patent holders benefit from clearer boundaries against leveraging patent licenses to foreclose competition in unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that bundle patent licenses with product purchase requirements face significant antitrust exposure, particularly where the tied products have uses outside the scope of the patent.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model are abstract under step one of Alice, and whether damages based on foreign sales of infringing software were properly awarded.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the judgment, holding that Columbia's asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents directed to detecting malicious software by comparing emulated function calls to behavioral models are abstract at Alice step one, requiring remand for step two analysis. The court also held that damages based on Norton's foreign sales were improperly awarded. The $185 million jury verdict was set aside.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against cybersecurity-related patent claims have a stronger basis to challenge eligibility under § 101, and foreign sales may be excluded from damages calculations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in software and cybersecurity fields face heightened risk that their claims will be deemed abstract, potentially invalidating valuable portfolios. Universities and research institutions may see reduced damages awards.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server tailoring content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods of tailoring streamed content specifications to device capabilities are ineligible under § 101 as directed to abstract ideas, reversing the district court's denial of Netflix's eligibility challenge and requiring entry of judgment for Netflix. The court also vacated rulings on inducement and damages evidence for potential future relevance.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies gain a significant precedent for defending against patents claiming adaptive content delivery to devices, a foundational concept in modern content distribution.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims covering server-side content adaptation face serious eligibility challenges, potentially rendering valuable streaming technology patents unenforceable.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment of inequitable conduct was proper where the patent applicant allegedly withheld prior art documents (CYMAX Documents) from the PTO, and whether summary judgment dismissing a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim was appropriate.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud, finding genuine disputes of material fact precluded resolution on either issue. Tenaris allegedly obtained patents on coiled tubing technology while withholding prior art documents describing similar technology it acquired from Southwestern Pipe. The case was remanded for trial on both issues.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct in patent prosecution benefit from the court's insistence on factual development before summary resolution. Companies pursuing Walker Process antitrust claims gain a pathway to trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquired prior art through corporate acquisitions face heightened litigation risk if disclosure obligations during prosecution are questioned, with potential exposure to both unenforceability and antitrust liability.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion by excluding a damages expert report for using an allegedly improper methodology and then granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft's Azure platform, finding the district court applied too stringent a standard under Daubert. The court vacated the summary judgment of no remedy and the denial of Exafer's motion to reopen discovery, remanding for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's guidance that damages expert reports should not be excluded merely because alternative methodologies exist, preserving plaintiffs' ability to present damages evidence at trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defendants relying on Daubert exclusions to obtain summary judgment face a higher bar, as the Federal Circuit signaled that district courts should be more permissive in allowing damages theories to reach juries.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion by excluding plaintiff's expert witnesses on induced infringement and then granting JMOL to the defendant in a medical device patent case.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Barry's expert witnesses and the resulting JMOL for DePuy in a case involving patents on spinal derotation surgical techniques. The court found the exclusions were an abuse of discretion and remanded for a new trial where both experts may testify on whether DePuy induced surgeons to infringe.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device space benefit from a more permissive standard for expert testimony on induced infringement, particularly where the alleged infringement involves surgeon use of devices.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed litigation exposure where expert exclusion previously provided a pathway to defense judgment, as the Federal Circuit signals closer scrutiny of Daubert rulings in patent cases.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities allowing asynchronous viewing of data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal for failure to state a claim, holding that the asserted web conferencing patent claims were ineligible under § 101. The claims, covering systems that allow participants to view recorded portions of a presentation while it continues live, were found directed to abstract ideas without an inventive concept.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies operating web conferencing and streaming platforms gain another precedent confirming that broad claims to known computing concepts like time-shifting and asynchronous playback are patent-ineligible.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities and holders of software patents covering web conferencing functionality face increased vulnerability to early-stage dismissal under § 101.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether means-plus-function claim terms in a crop harvester height control patent were indefinite where the specification described the corresponding structure as 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted claims of the '395 patent were indefinite, holding that the specification's description of the 'control means' structure—including controller interface, head controller, and hydraulic control system—provided sufficient structure even though it was described as 'conventional.' The case was remanded for further proceedings on infringement.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's holding that describing corresponding structure as 'conventional' does not automatically render means-plus-function claims indefinite, preserving enforceability of agricultural and industrial technology patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defendants in patent cases face reduced ability to invalidate means-plus-function claims on indefiniteness grounds where the specification identifies structural components, even if described at a high level.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing how the Board exercises discretion to decline IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and the Director retains ultimate authority over non-institution decisions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued flexibility to manage IPR institution decisions without being constrained by formal rulemaking processes, potentially resulting in fewer IPR institutions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners lose their challenge to discretionary denial practices, leaving the PTO Director broad, largely unreviewable authority to decline institution of patent challenges.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute and whether the CIT properly applied the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the CIT's holding that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute governing confidentiality in trade remedy proceedings. The court held that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records and that confidentiality determinations must be made on a case-by-case basis consistent with statutory requirements.
impacts.positiveImplications: Domestic industries and the public benefit from increased transparency in trade remedy proceedings, as the ITC can no longer shield all questionnaire data from judicial scrutiny through blanket confidentiality designations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Foreign manufacturers and importers who submit sensitive business data in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk of disclosure, potentially chilling cooperation with ITC questionnaires.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the OPM regulation requiring prior written authorization for federal employee overtime (5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c)) is a valid exercise of rulemaking authority under 5 U.S.C. § 5548(a), given the statutory requirement to pay for overtime 'officially ordered or approved' under 5 U.S.C. § 5542(a).
impacts.description: Sitting en banc, the Federal Circuit held that OPM's longstanding writing requirement for overtime authorization is a valid exercise of delegated rulemaking authority. The court affirmed dismissal of Count II of the complaint brought by IHS nurses who worked extensive unrecorded overtime during COVID-19. However, the court remanded counts involving other pay enhancement claims for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Federal agencies benefit from the validation of the 80-year-old writing requirement for overtime, which provides a clear administrative mechanism to control overtime costs and liability.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal employees, particularly healthcare workers who performed substantial unrecorded overtime during the pandemic, face a significant barrier to recovering overtime pay absent prior written authorization, even where supervisors were aware of the work.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether IHS registered nurses hired under Title 5 but paid under Title 38 base pay provisions are entitled to enhanced compensation for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under 38 U.S.C. § 7453 and related regulations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit panel reversed dismissal of Counts I and III-V, holding that IHS nurses plausibly alleged entitlement to pay enhancements under Title 38 provisions applicable to Title 5 healthcare employees. The court found that the statutory framework permits application of Title 38 compensation provisions beyond base pay to eligible Title 5 healthcare workers, and remanded for further proceedings on these claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies face potential backpay liability for failing to apply Title 38 enhanced compensation provisions (overtime, nighttime, Sunday, holiday pay) to Title 5 nurses and similar healthcare employees, with potential class-wide exposure.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute (15 U.S.C. § 9009c) creates a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act where the SBA failed to award grants in the order applications were received.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants stated a viable Tucker Act claim for unpaid RRF grants. The court held that the statute's directive that the SBA 'shall award grants' in the order applications were received created a money-mandating obligation, giving the Court of Federal Claims jurisdiction over claims by applicants who were bypassed despite applying before other entities that received grants.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses that applied for but did not receive federal pandemic relief grants may pursue monetary claims against the government where the statute imposed mandatory grant-making obligations and the agency failed to follow statutory priorities.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability to hundreds of restaurants that were passed over for RRF grants, establishing a precedent that mandatory statutory language in federal grant programs can create enforceable payment obligations.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly found no violation of Section 337 based on lack of likelihood of confusion for Crocs's 3D trade dress marks, and whether the ITC properly entered only a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents rather than a general exclusion order.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's appeal of the no-violation finding against active respondents as untimely and affirmed the ITC's limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents. Under § 1337(g)(1), the ITC properly presumed the complaint's allegations true against defaulters and issued an LEO, but was not required to issue the broader general exclusion order Crocs sought.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and competitors benefit from confirmation that trade dress holders cannot obtain general exclusion orders based solely on default proceedings and must timely appeal adverse ITC findings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners relying on 3D trade dress marks to block imports face limitations on the scope of relief available in ITC proceedings, particularly the difficulty of obtaining general exclusion orders against non-participating foreign manufacturers.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the jury's finding of nonobviousness of a patent covering pre-lit artificial trees with coaxial trunk connectors was supported by substantial evidence, and whether the damages award was proper.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed denial of Polygroup's JMOL of obviousness and motion for new trial on damages for Willis's patent covering pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical and electrical connections. The court upheld the jury's finding that the prior art did not render the coaxial connector claims obvious and affirmed the damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer products benefit from the court's deference to jury findings on nonobviousness where the invention addresses a practical problem with an integrated solution, even in seemingly simple product categories.
impacts.negativeImplications: Consumer product competitors face continued patent enforcement risk where patents cover mechanical/electrical integration features, with limited ability to overturn jury verdicts on obviousness.
